events.text: The patient is a 71-year-old male named Raymond Kowalski. He is of Polish-American descent, a retired ironworker, non-smoker, with a family history notable for his father dying of prostate cancer at age 78 and a paternal aunt with ovarian cancer. He has a BMI of 26.4 and an ECOG performance status of 1 at the time of initial diagnosis.

At age 68 years, the patient had a diagnosis of Stage IVB (AJCC 8th edition) ADENOCARCINOMA OF THE PROSTATE (ICD-O-3 site code C61.9, histology code 8140/3). The Gleason score was 9 (5+4), Grade Group 5. Clinical T3bN1M1b (bone metastases). PSA at diagnosis was 187.4 ng/mL. Summary stage: Distant. Relevant site-specific factors included: lymph node involvement (pelvic LN positive by biopsy), bone scan showing lesions in lumbar spine (L3, L4) and right iliac crest. Laterality: Not applicable (prostate). Confirmation: POSITIVE HISTOLOGY via transrectal ultrasound-guided biopsy (12 cores, 10/12 positive). Tumor Size: Not applicable (diffuse involvement). Perineural invasion: present. Lymphovascular invasion: present.

At age 68 years, the patient had a pathology result of type ANATOMIC PATHOLOGY (transrectal ultrasound-guided needle core biopsy). Specimen: 12 core prostate needle biopsy. Diagnosis: Prostatic adenocarcinoma, Gleason score 9 (5+4), Grade Group 5. 10 of 12 cores positive. Greatest percentage core involvement: 95% (right apex). Perineural invasion: present. Lymphovascular invasion: present. No seminal vesicle tissue present in cores. IHC: PSA positive, PSAP positive, NKX3.1 positive, p63 negative, HMWCK negative, consistent with prostatic primary. Pathologic stage (biopsy): pT2 (clinical T3b based on imaging).

At age 68 years, the patient had a Tc-99m bone scintigraphy (whole body bone scan), which showed cancer. Metastatic sites identified: increased tracer uptake at L3 vertebral body, L4 vertebral body, right iliac crest, and right sacral ala, consistent with osseous metastatic disease. No evidence of superscan. CT chest/abdomen/pelvis performed concurrently showed: enlarged right external iliac lymph nodes (largest 1.8 cm), no visceral metastases (lung/liver negative), prostate gland enlarged at 4.8 cm with extracapsular extension and possible seminal vesicle involvement. Impression: Stage IVB prostate adenocarcinoma with bone and lymph node metastases, no visceral metastases.

At age 68 years, the patient initiated androgen deprivation therapy (ADT) with leuprolide acetate 22.5 mg intramuscular injection every 3 months (luteinizing hormone-releasing hormone agonist) combined with abiraterone acetate 1000 mg orally daily plus prednisone 5 mg orally twice daily for metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer (mHSPC). Treatment intent: life-prolonging (palliative). A bicalutamide flare prophylaxis (50 mg daily for 4 weeks) was administered at ADT initiation.

At age 68 years (3 months after starting ADT + abiraterone), the patient had an oncologist office assessment. PSA declined from 187.4 to 4.2 ng/mL (97.8% decline). Testosterone was castrate at <10 ng/dL. Bone pain improved significantly (patient reports 2/10 pain vs. 7/10 at diagnosis). ECOG PS: 1. Tolerating abiraterone well with no significant fluid retention or hypokalemia. Blood pressure well controlled. No cancer progression identified. Response assessment: major PSA response.

At age 69 years (12 months after starting ADT + abiraterone), the patient had an oncologist office assessment. PSA nadir reached at 0.8 ng/mL. Testosterone castrate at <10 ng/dL. Patient reports fatigue (grade 1), no bone pain. ECOG PS: 1. Repeat bone scan showed no new lesions; known lesions stable/sclerotic (healing response). Continuing current regimen. Referral placed for germline genetic counseling given family history.

At age 69 years, the patient had a pathology result of type GERMLINE GENETIC TESTING (peripheral blood). Specimen: peripheral blood DNA. Testing platform: comprehensive germline cancer panel (84-gene panel). Result: PATHOGENIC VARIANT IDENTIFIED - BRCA2 c.5946delT (p.Ser1982Argfs*22), a well-characterized frameshift pathogenic variant. Zygosity: heterozygous. Additional findings: ATM wild-type, CDK12 wild-type, CHEK2 wild-type. Interpretation: This germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant confers significantly increased risk for prostate, breast, ovarian, and pancreatic cancers. Family members at 50% risk; cascade genetic testing recommended. Genetic counselor note: Patient informed of implications for self and family members.

At age 69 years (18 months after starting ADT + abiraterone), the patient had a Tc-99m bone scintigraphy, which showed cancer (stable). Known lesions at L3, L4, right iliac crest and right sacral ala show sclerotic changes consistent with treated/healing bone metastases. No new lesions identified. CT chest/abdomen/pelvis: No visceral metastases. Right external iliac lymph nodes decreased in size (now 0.9 cm). PSA at time of scan: 1.1 ng/mL (rising from nadir of 0.8). Impression: Radiographically stable disease; PSA beginning to rise from nadir.

At age 70 years (24 months after starting ADT + abiraterone), the patient had an oncologist office assessment showing rising PSA consistent with castration-resistant prostate cancer (CRPC). PSA now 8.6 ng/mL (three consecutive rises above nadir, with PSA doubling time of approximately 3.5 months). Testosterone confirmed castrate at <10 ng/dL. Patient reports new low back pain (3/10). ECOG PS: 1. Diagnosis: Metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC). Decision: Transition to enzalutamide monotherapy (continuing LHRH agonist for castrate testosterone maintenance). Rationale: BRCA2-mutant tumor; however, given prior response to AR-pathway therapy and patient preference to defer chemotherapy, enzalutamide chosen. PARP inhibitor (olaparib) deferred as second-line option.

At age 70 years, the patient discontinued abiraterone/prednisone and initiated enzalutamide 160 mg orally daily for metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (second-line AR-pathway inhibitor). Leuprolide acetate continued at 22.5 mg every 3 months to maintain castrate testosterone levels. Treatment intent: life-prolonging (palliative). Baseline PSA at enzalutamide start: 8.6 ng/mL.

At age 70 years, the patient had next generation sequencing performed for metastatic castration-resistant prostate adenocarcinoma based on a specimen obtained from a CT-guided biopsy of the L3 vertebral metastasis (bone, decalcified). Somatic tumor profile: BRCA2 loss (deep deletion, biallelic loss consistent with LOH of germline allele and somatic deletion of second allele - consistent with second-hit mechanism), TP53 p.R175H (pathogenic), AR amplification (copy number gain ~8x), RB1 loss (homozygous deletion), CDK12 wild-type, PTEN loss (heterozygous), MYC amplification (copy number ~4x). Fusions: none detected. MSI status: MSS (microsatellite stable). TMB: 4 mutations/Mb (low). PD-L1 expression: 1% (low). Actionable alterations: BRCA2 biallelic loss (PARP inhibitor eligible - olaparib FDA-approved indication), AR amplification (enzalutamide resistance mechanism). HRR gene panel: BRCA1 wild-type, BRCA2 biallelic loss (pathogenic), ATM wild-type, CDK12 wild-type, PALB2 wild-type, RAD51B wild-type, FANCA wild-type. Tumor purity: 60%.

At age 70 years (3 months after starting enzalutamide), the patient had an oncologist office assessment showing PSA response to enzalutamide. PSA declined from 8.6 to 2.3 ng/mL (73% decline). Patient reports improvement in back pain (1/10). ECOG PS: 1. Adverse effects: grade 1 fatigue, grade 1 hot flashes (pre-existing from ADT). No seizures or neurologic events. Bone scan deferred per protocol (next imaging at 6 months). Cancer status: responding to therapy.

At age 70 years (6 months after starting enzalutamide), the patient had a Tc-99m bone scintigraphy and CT chest/abdomen/pelvis, which showed cancer (stable on bone scan, no visceral metastases). Bone scan: known L3, L4, right iliac crest lesions stable without new lesions. CT: no visceral metastases (liver, lung, adrenal glands clear). No new lymphadenopathy. PSA at time of scan: 2.8 ng/mL (slight rise from nadir of 1.9 ng/mL but not clearly progressive by PCWG3 criteria). Impression: Stable osseous metastatic disease; PSA beginning to trend upward from nadir.

At age 70 years (6 months after starting enzalutamide), the patient had an oncologist office assessment. PSA rising to 4.1 ng/mL; PSA doubling time approximately 2.8 months. Testosterone castrate. Patient reports worsening back pain (4/10) and new right hip pain. ECOG PS: 1-2. Urgent bone scan ordered. Assessment: Early enzalutamide progression by PSA criteria; clinical and radiographic confirmation pending. Plan: Proceed with confirmatory imaging; if confirmed progression, transition to olaparib given BRCA2 biallelic loss (FDA-approved indication per PROfound trial data).

At age 70 years (7 months after starting enzalutamide), the patient had a Tc-99m bone scintigraphy (confirmatory), which showed cancer with new bone metastases (progressive disease). New areas of increased tracer uptake identified at: right femoral neck/intertrochanteric region, T11 vertebral body, and left acetabulum. Previously known lesions at L3, L4, right iliac crest, and right sacral ala appear larger. PSMA PET/CT (Ga-68 PSMA-11) also performed: confirmed all bone lesions PSMA-avid, additionally identified 2 small new PSMA-avid right pelvic lymph nodes (largest 0.8 cm). No visceral metastases on PSMA PET. SUVmax of L3 lesion: 18.4. Impression: Progressive metastatic CRPC with new bone metastases (radiographic progression per PCWG3), no visceral disease.

At age 70 years (7 months after enzalutamide start), the patient discontinued enzalutamide and initiated olaparib 300 mg orally twice daily for metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer with BRCA2 biallelic loss (FDA-approved, PROfound trial indication). Leuprolide acetate continued for testosterone suppression. Denosumab 120 mg subcutaneously every 4 weeks also initiated for bone metastasis-related skeletal events prevention (with calcium 500 mg and vitamin D 400 IU supplementation). Zoledronic acid was not chosen due to renal function considerations (GFR 52 mL/min). Treatment intent: life-prolonging (palliative).

At age 71 years (3 months after starting olaparib), the patient had an oncologist office assessment showing PSA response to olaparib. PSA declined from 18.2 (at olaparib start) to 5.7 ng/mL (69% decline). Patient reports significant improvement in bone pain (1-2/10). ECOG PS: 1. Adverse effects: Grade 2 fatigue, Grade 1 nausea (improving with ondansetron PRN). CBC: Hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL (Grade 1 anemia, baseline Hgb was 12.4 before olaparib). No thrombocytopenia. Creatinine stable. Monitoring plan: CBC every 4 weeks, comprehensive metabolic panel monthly. Cancer status: responding to PARP inhibitor therapy.

At age 71 years (4 months after starting olaparib), the patient experienced Grade 2 anemia (hemoglobin 9.1 g/dL, decline from baseline 12.4 g/dL). Patient reports increased fatigue (Grade 2), dyspnea on exertion (Grade 1), and reduced exercise tolerance. Reticulocyte count low (0.4%), MCV mildly elevated at 98 fL, consistent with olaparib-associated bone marrow suppression (megaloblastic pattern). Iron studies: serum iron 68 mcg/dL, ferritin 280 ng/mL, TIBC normal - no iron deficiency. Vitamin B12 and folate normal. Assessment: Olaparib-related Grade 2 anemia. Management: Olaparib dose reduced to 200 mg orally twice daily per protocol. EPO-stimulating agents not initiated at this time. Supportive care: folic acid supplementation, activity modification counseling. Next CBC in 2 weeks.

At age 71 years (6 months after starting olaparib), the patient had a Tc-99m bone scintigraphy and CT chest/abdomen/pelvis, which showed cancer (stable/partial response in bone). Bone scan: Previously new lesions at right femoral neck, T11, and left acetabulum show reduced tracer uptake consistent with treatment response. Known lesions at L3, L4, right iliac crest stable/sclerotic. No new bone lesions. CT chest/abdomen/pelvis: No visceral metastases. Previously identified right pelvic lymph nodes decreased to 0.4 cm. PSA at time of scan: 3.9 ng/mL. Impression: Partial radiographic response to olaparib in bone metastases; no visceral disease.

At age 71 years (7 months after starting olaparib), the patient experienced Grade 3 anemia (hemoglobin 7.4 g/dL). Patient symptomatic with fatigue (Grade 3), exertional dyspnea (Grade 2), lightheadedness. Heart rate 104 bpm, BP 118/74 mmHg. CBC: WBC 4.2 K/uL, platelets 128 K/uL (Grade 1 thrombocytopenia). Reticulocyte count 0.3%. Assessment: Grade 3 anemia requiring blood transfusion. Olaparib held pending hemoglobin recovery. Management: Packed red blood cell transfusion administered (2 units pRBC); post-transfusion Hgb 9.8 g/dL. Patient monitored in infusion center. Plan: Resume olaparib at reduced dose (150 mg twice daily) once Hgb >9 g/dL and stable.

At age 71 years (8 months after starting olaparib), the patient had an oncologist office assessment following anemia episode and transfusion. Hemoglobin 9.6 g/dL post-transfusion and dose hold. PSA: 4.4 ng/mL (slight rise during dose hold, not clearly progressive). Olaparib resumed at 150 mg twice daily. Patient fatigue improved to Grade 1 after transfusion. ECOG PS: 1. Erythropoiesis-stimulating agent (darbepoetin alfa) initiated per oncologist discretion for transfusion-dependent anemia in the setting of non-myelosuppressive cancer therapy context. Plan: Darbepoetin alfa 500 mcg subcutaneous every 3 weeks; CBC monitoring every 3 weeks. Hemoglobin target 10-12 g/dL. Cancer status: disease present, on active therapy with partial response.

At age 71 years (9 months after starting olaparib), the patient experienced a second episode of Grade 3 anemia (hemoglobin 7.1 g/dL) despite darbepoetin alfa support. Additionally noted Grade 2 thrombocytopenia (platelets 68 K/uL). Assessment: Progressive myelosuppression on olaparib 150 mg BID despite dose reduction. Patient received second packed red blood cell transfusion (2 units pRBC), post-transfusion Hgb 9.4 g/dL. Platelets monitored (no transfusion required). Olaparib held again. Hematology consultation obtained; bone marrow biopsy deferred given clinical context (PARP inhibitor-associated myelosuppression most likely). Plan: Consider further dose reduction to 100 mg BID vs. permanent discontinuation pending clinical assessment.

At age 71 years (11 months after starting olaparib), the patient had a PSMA PET/CT (Ga-68 PSMA-11) and CT chest/abdomen/pelvis (dedicated), which showed cancer with new visceral metastases (progressive disease). PSMA PET findings: New PSMA-avid hepatic lesion in right lobe (segment VII, 1.4 cm, SUVmax 8.9). New PSMA-avid left lower lobe pulmonary nodule (0.9 cm, SUVmax 6.2). Progressive enlargement of previously known bone lesions with 3 new osseous lesions (right femur shaft, left posterior rib 6, C7 vertebral body). Pelvic lymph nodes re-enlarged (right external iliac 1.6 cm). CT chest/abdomen/pelvis confirmed: hepatic lesion 1.4 cm in right lobe segment VII (new), pulmonary nodule left lower lobe 0.9 cm (new). PSA at time of imaging: 31.7 ng/mL. Impression: Visceral metastatic progression (liver and lung) with progressive osseous and nodal disease. BRCA2-mutant mCRPC with visceral progression on PARP inhibitor therapy.

At age 71 years (11 months after starting olaparib), the patient had an oncologist office assessment confirming radiographic progression with visceral metastases on olaparib. PSA 31.7 ng/mL (rising). ECOG PS: 2 (worsening from baseline). Patient reports new right upper quadrant discomfort (2/10), persistent fatigue (Grade 2), 8-lb unintentional weight loss over 3 months. CBC: Hgb 8.8 g/dL (post-transfusion, last transfusion 3 weeks prior), platelets 92 K/uL, WBC 3.8 K/uL. LFTs: ALT 48 U/L (mildly elevated), AST 52 U/L (mildly elevated), bilirubin normal. Assessment: Progressive mCRPC with visceral metastases (liver, lung) on PARP inhibitor therapy. Olaparib discontinued. Discussion with patient regarding goals of care, performance status, and treatment options. Options discussed: platinum-based chemotherapy (carboplatin + docetaxel or cabazitaxel), VISION trial-eligible for Lu-177 PSMA if PSMA-positive (confirmed on recent scan), clinical trial enrollment, or transition to best supportive care. Patient wishes to pursue further treatment. Plan: Refer for palliative care consultation; proceed with carboplatin + docetaxel chemotherapy given visceral crisis risk.

At age 71 years, the patient discontinued olaparib and initiated platinum-based combination chemotherapy with docetaxel 60 mg/m2 IV day 1 plus carboplatin AUC 4 IV day 1 every 21 days for progressive metastatic CRPC with visceral metastases and BRCA2 biallelic loss (carboplatin chosen given platinum sensitivity expected in HRR-deficient tumors, per retrospective data and BRCA2 germline mutation status). LHRH agonist (leuprolide) continued. G-CSF (filgrastim) prophylaxis given with each cycle (dose-dense schedule, ECOG PS 2, prior myelosuppression history). Pre-medications: dexamethasone 8 mg IV, ondansetron 8 mg IV. Treatment intent: palliative, life-prolonging.

At age 71 years (during cycle 1 of carboplatin + docetaxel), the patient experienced Grade 3 neutropenia (ANC 0.6 K/uL on day 12), managed with filgrastim support (already on prophylactic G-CSF). Patient did not require hospitalization (afebrile, no infectious symptoms). Additionally: Grade 2 peripheral neuropathy (numbness/tingling bilateral feet, worsening from Grade 1 pre-existing from prior taxane exposure). Grade 2 alopecia (patient counseled at start of therapy). Nausea Grade 2 (controlled with ondansetron + prochlorperazine). Docetaxel dose reduced to 55 mg/m2 for cycle 2 due to neutropenia and neuropathy. Carboplatin dose maintained.

At age 71 years (after 2 cycles of carboplatin + docetaxel), the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis, which showed cancer with mixed response. Hepatic lesion in segment VII: decreased from 1.4 cm to 0.8 cm (partial response). Pulmonary nodule left lower lobe: stable at 0.9 cm. Bone lesions: stable to minimally improved. Pelvic lymph nodes: decreased (right external iliac now 1.0 cm). PSA at time of imaging: 18.4 ng/mL (declined from 31.7). Impression: Mixed response to platinum-doublet chemotherapy; hepatic metastasis responding, pulmonary lesion stable. PSA declining. Plan: Continue chemotherapy with response assessment after cycle 4.

At age 71 years (after 3 cycles of carboplatin + docetaxel), the patient had an oncologist office assessment. PSA 12.1 ng/mL (continued decline, 62% from pre-chemotherapy peak). ECOG PS: 2. Tolerating chemotherapy with dose reduction in place. Cumulative peripheral neuropathy now Grade 2 bilaterally in feet and hands. CBC: Hgb 9.2 g/dL (stable, last transfusion 6 weeks ago), platelets 102 K/uL, WBC 5.4 K/uL (on filgrastim). Right upper quadrant discomfort resolved. Weight stable. Patient remains engaged in goals of care discussions. Palliative care team actively involved. Supportive care: physical therapy for neuropathy, occupational therapy referral. Cancer status: disease present, partial response to chemotherapy. Continued need for close monitoring given ECOG PS 2 and cumulative treatment toxicity.

At age 71 years (after 4 cycles of carboplatin + docetaxel), the patient had an oncologist office assessment with PSA re-elevation suggesting resistance emerging. PSA 19.8 ng/mL (rising after initial decline). ECOG PS: 2-3 (worsening functional status, requires assistance with some ADLs). New symptom: Grade 2 fatigue, Grade 2-3 peripheral neuropathy limiting ambulation. Patient and family meeting held with palliative care team, oncologist, and social worker. Discussion regarding treatment goals, burden of therapy, and quality of life. Patient expresses desire to continue treatment if tolerable but prioritizes quality of life and time at home. Assessment: Rising PSA after 4 cycles carboplatin/docetaxel with worsening PS and cumulative toxicity. Plan: Obtain imaging to assess extent of progression. Given PSMA-positive disease on prior imaging, discuss Lu-177-PSMA-617 therapy (VISION trial FDA approved indication) if performance status permits, vs. clinical trial vs. best supportive care. Radium-223 not indicated given visceral metastases. Cabazitaxel considered but limited by PS and prior neuropathy.

At age 71 years (restaging after 4 cycles of carboplatin + docetaxel), the patient had a PSMA PET/CT (Ga-68 PSMA-11) and CT chest/abdomen/pelvis, which showed cancer with progressive disease. Hepatic lesions: prior segment VII lesion re-enlarged (now 2.1 cm) and 2 new hepatic lesions (segments II and V, 1.0 cm and 0.7 cm). Pulmonary nodule: stable. Bone lesions: multiple new lesions (thoracic spine T5, T8; sacrum; bilateral pubic rami). Pelvic lymph nodes: progressive enlargement. PSMA uptake: all lesions strongly PSMA-avid (liver SUVmax 12.4, bone lesions SUVmax 14-21). No salivary gland exclusion concerns. PSMA PET eligibility for Lu-177 therapy: PSMA-positive tumor burden (all metastatic sites PSMA-avid). Impression: Widespread progressive mCRPC with visceral (liver) and bone progression, PSMA-avid disease suitable for Lu-177-PSMA-617 consideration.

At age 71 years, the patient discontinued carboplatin + docetaxel chemotherapy after 4 cycles due to disease progression and declining performance status (ECOG PS 3). After multidisciplinary discussion including nuclear medicine, medical oncology, palliative care, and the patient/family, the patient was enrolled in lutetium-177 PSMA-617 (Lu-177-PSMA-617; Pluvicto) therapy given strongly PSMA-avid progressive disease (post-AR pathway therapy and post-taxane chemotherapy). Cycle 1 of Lu-177-PSMA-617 7.4 GBq IV administered. Leuprolide acetate continued. Hydration protocol implemented. Pre-treatment salivary gland protection (cooling) utilized. Antiemetics given prophylactically. Treatment intent: palliative, life-prolonging. Patient and family extensively counseled on realistic expectations, supportive care, and advance care planning.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CLINICAL SUMMARY
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Mr. Kowalski is a 70-year-old male with a known history of stage IVB metastatic hormone-sensitive prostatic adenocarcinoma (Gleason 9, Grade Group 5; cT3bN1M1b at diagnosis) initially presenting with bone metastases at L3, L4, right iliac crest, and right sacral ala, along with pelvic lymphadenopathy. He was treated with androgen deprivation therapy (leuprolide acetate) in combination with abiraterone acetate plus prednisone, achieving a major PSA response (nadir 0.8 ng/mL). Germline testing performed during this period revealed a pathogenic BRCA2 frameshift variant (c.5946delT, p.Ser1982Argfs*22). After approximately 24 months on first-line therapy, PSA progression consistent with castration-resistant prostate cancer (CRPC) was identified (PSA 8.6 ng/mL with documented castrate testosterone). This biopsy was obtained from the L3 vertebral metastasis at the time of confirmed mCRPC to characterize the somatic tumor landscape and guide subsequent therapy selection.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
GENOMIC FINDINGS SUMMARY
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

--- TIER I: VARIANTS WITH STRONG CLINICAL SIGNIFICANCE ---
(Established biomarker with direct FDA-approved therapeutic relevance or established prognostic significance)

1. BRCA2 — Biallelic Loss (Deep Deletion; Loss of Heterozygosity of Germline Allele + Somatic Second Hit)
   Variant Classification: Pathogenic — Tier I (Therapeutic)
   Genomic Coordinates: Chr13q12.3
   Mechanism: Homozygous functional loss via somatic deletion of the wild-type allele superimposed on the known germline pathogenic frameshift variant (c.5946delT; p.Ser1982Argfs*22). Copy number analysis demonstrates deep deletion of the BRCA2 locus with loss of heterozygosity (LOH), consistent with classic "second-hit" tumor suppressor inactivation.
   Allele Fraction: Not quantifiable (copy number loss mechanism; no somatic point mutation at this allele)
   Copy Number: <0.5 (deep deletion)
   Interpretation: Biallelic loss of BRCA2 results in complete disruption of homologous recombination repair (HRR) function. This renders the tumor hyperdependent on PARP-mediated DNA repair and constitutes a positive predictive biomarker for PARP inhibitor (PARPi) sensitivity. This finding fulfills the genomic eligibility criterion for olaparib (Lynparza) per FDA approval in mCRPC with BRCA1/2 mutations (PROfound trial, Hussain et al., NEJM 2020). Rucaparib (Rubraca) is also FDA-approved for BRCA1/2-mutant mCRPC (TRITON2 trial).
   Notes: Consistent with germline-somatic interaction. Germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant previously confirmed (peripheral blood; c.5946delT, p.Ser1982Argfs*22, heterozygous). Somatic loss of the second allele in tumor tissue confirms complete HRR deficiency.

2. AR — Amplification (Copy Number Gain ~8x; High-Level Amplification)
   Variant Classification: Pathogenic — Tier I (Resistance/Prognostic)
   Genomic Coordinates: ChrXq12
   Copy Number: ~8x (relative to ploidy-adjusted diploid baseline)
   Interpretation: High-level AR amplification is a well-characterized mechanism of resistance to androgen receptor pathway inhibitors (ARPIs), including abiraterone acetate and enzalutamide. This finding is consistent with the clinical observation of disease progression following approximately 24 months of abiraterone-based therapy. AR amplification results in increased AR protein expression, enabling continued AR signaling even at castrate androgen levels and in the presence of AR antagonists. This finding has direct implications for enzalutamide (currently initiated): the presence of high-level AR amplification at mCRPC transition predicts a potentially limited duration of second-line ARPI benefit, though some patients with AR amplification still derive meaningful responses to enzalutamide. Alternative AR-targeted approaches or early transition to non-AR-directed therapies may warrant consideration if enzalutamide response is limited.
   Notes: AR amplification is present in approximately 40-60% of mCRPC cases. This copy number gain may have emerged during prior abiraterone/ADT exposure (selection pressure) and is the most common genomic mechanism of ARPI resistance in this disease context.

--- TIER II: VARIANTS WITH POTENTIAL CLINICAL SIGNIFICANCE ---
(Variants with emerging evidence for therapeutic relevance or established prognostic significance in this cancer type)

3. TP53 — p.R175H (c.524G>A)
   Variant Classification: Pathogenic — Tier II (Prognostic; potential therapeutic relevance)
   Allele Fraction: 54% (consistent with tumor purity ~60%; likely clonal)
   Exon: 5
   Variant Type: Missense (hotspot gain-of-function mutation)
   Interpretation: TP53 p.R175H is one of the most frequently occurring TP53 hotspot mutations and results in a structural conformational change in the DNA-binding domain, leading to loss of wild-type tumor suppressor function and acquisition of oncogenic gain-of-function properties (dominant-negative inhibition of residual wild-type p53 activity; upregulation of pro-survival transcriptional programs). TP53 mutations are present in approximately 40% of mCRPC cases and are associated with aggressive disease biology, shorter responses to AR-directed therapies, and inferior overall survival. The clonal nature of this variant (high allele fraction) suggests it was an early driver event. No direct FDA-approved therapies target TP53 p.R175H in prostate cancer at this time; however, ongoing trials are evaluating p53-directed strategies.
   Notes: Co-occurrence of TP53 loss-of-function and BRCA2 biallelic loss in the same tumor is well-described and may further amplify genomic instability through defective G1/S and G2/M DNA damage checkpoints.

4. RB1 — Homozygous Deletion
   Variant Classification: Pathogenic — Tier II (Prognostic; lineage plasticity marker)
   Genomic Coordinates: Chr13q14.2
   Copy Number: <0.5 (homozygous deletion)
   Interpretation: RB1 loss is a critical event in the evolution of aggressive prostate cancer variants, including neuroendocrine differentiation and treatment-induced small cell/neuroendocrine prostate cancer (t-SCNC). Concurrent RB1 loss and TP53 mutation is a recognized genomic signature predisposing to lineage plasticity and AR-independent disease progression. While the current histology remains adenocarcinoma (as per the biopsy submitted), the co-occurrence of biallelic RB1 loss and TP53 mutation warrants vigilance for phenotypic conversion to neuroendocrine histology during subsequent therapy, particularly with continued AR-pathway inhibitor exposure. Clinical and pathologic monitoring for SCNC features (e.g., neuroendocrine markers, loss of AR expression, rapidly rising NSE/chromogranin) is recommended.
   Notes: RB1 locus is co-localized with BRCA2 on chromosome 13q; concurrent deletions affecting both loci are observed in a subset of BRCA2-deleted prostate cancers.

5. MYC — Amplification (Copy Number ~4x)
   Variant Classification: Potentially Pathogenic — Tier II (Prognostic)
   Genomic Coordinates: Chr8q24.21
   Copy Number: ~4x (moderate amplification)
   Interpretation: MYC amplification is found in approximately 20-30% of mCRPC cases and is associated with increased cell proliferation, anti-apoptotic signaling, and metabolic reprogramming. MYC amplification cooperates with AR pathway dysregulation to drive prostate cancer progression. No direct FDA-approved MYC inhibitors are available at this time, though BET bromodomain inhibitors targeting MYC transcription are in clinical development. This finding has prognostic significance.

6. PTEN — Heterozygous Loss (Single Copy Loss)
   Variant Classification: Potentially Pathogenic — Tier II (Prognostic; pathway relevance)
   Genomic Coordinates: Chr10q23.31
   Copy Number: ~1 (single copy loss; heterozygous)
   Interpretation: PTEN is a tumor suppressor that negatively regulates the PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway. Heterozygous PTEN loss is observed in approximately 40-50% of prostate cancers and is associated with PI3K pathway activation, increased cell survival, and reduced sensitivity to certain therapies. Complete homozygous PTEN loss (not detected here) is associated with more pronounced PI3K activation. The heterozygous deletion observed here represents haploinsufficiency and may contribute to partial PI3K pathway deregulation. Ipatasertib (AKT inhibitor) and copanlisib have been evaluated in PTEN-altered prostate cancer; these are not currently standard of care but may be of interest in clinical trial settings. No immediate actionable implication for approved therapies at this time.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

FDA-APPROVED THERAPIES (This Cancer Type — Prostate Adenocarcinoma)

  [1] BRCA2 Biallelic Loss → PARP INHIBITOR ELIGIBLE
      - Olaparib (Lynparza) 300 mg PO BID — FDA-approved for BRCA1/2-mutated mCRPC (PROfound trial; Hussain et al., NEJM 2020)
      - Rucaparib (Rubraca) 600 mg PO BID — FDA-approved for BRCA1/2-mutated mCRPC (TRITON2/3)
      - Note: BRCA2 biallelic loss (germline + somatic second hit) is the strongest predictive biomarker for PARP inhibitor response in mCRPC; median radiographic PFS benefit of approximately 7.4 months over physician's choice of abiraterone or enzalutamide in the PROfound cohort A.
      - Combination strategies under investigation: Olaparib + enzalutamide (PROpel trial), niraparib + abiraterone (MAGNITUDE trial) — combination data available but single-agent olaparib remains the established reference.

  [2] AR Amplification → AR Pathway Inhibitor Resistance Mechanism
      - High-level AR amplification (~8x) is associated with reduced duration of benefit from second-line AR-pathway inhibitors (enzalutamide, abiraterone).
      - Current therapy (enzalutamide) may still provide transient benefit; however, early progression on enzalutamide should prompt reconsideration of PARP inhibitor sequencing.
      - AR splice variants (AR-V7) were not directly detected at protein level (IHC not performed); however, AR amplification and splicing dysregulation frequently co-occur.

FDA-APPROVED THERAPIES (Other Tumor Types — Off-Label Consideration or Clinical Trial Context)

  [3] BRCA2 Biallelic Loss → Platinum Chemotherapy Sensitivity
      - HRR-deficient tumors, particularly BRCA2-mutant cancers, demonstrate increased sensitivity to DNA cross-linking agents (cisplatin, carboplatin). Carboplatin-based regimens (e.g., carboplatin + docetaxel or carboplatin + cabazitaxel) have shown activity in BRCA-mutated mCRPC in retrospective and small prospective series.
      - This is NOT an FDA-approved indication but represents a clinically relevant option in platinum-naive HRR-deficient mCRPC, particularly if PARPi options have been exhausted.

  [4] MSS / TMB-Low / PD-L1 Low → Immune Checkpoint Inhibitors NOT Indicated by Biomarker Profile
      - Pembrolizumab (Keytruda) is NOT supported by current biomarker results (MSS, TMB-Low, PD-L1 CPS 1).

NO FDA-APPROVED TARGETED THERAPIES FOR:
  - TP53 p.R175H (no approved p53-directed therapies in prostate cancer)
  - RB1 homozygous deletion (no approved therapies; prognostic/lineage plasticity marker)
  - MYC amplification (no approved MYC inhibitors)
  - PTEN heterozygous loss (PI3K/AKT pathway inhibitors investigational in prostate cancer)

CLINICAL TRIAL CONSIDERATIONS:
  - TRITON3 / MAGNITUDE / PROpel combination strategies for BRCA2-mutant mCRPC
  - BET bromodomain inhibitor trials (targeting MYC amplification)
  - AKT inhibitor trials in PTEN-altered prostate cancer
  - p53-directed immunotherapy trials

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
This genomic profile is consistent with a highly aggressive, treatment-selected mCRPC landscape. The combination of biallelic BRCA2 loss, TP53 hotspot mutation (p.R175H), RB1 homozygous deletion, AR amplification, and MYC amplification represents a genomically complex tumor with multiple co-occurring driver alterations.

Key interpretive points:

1. BRCA2 Second-Hit Confirmation: The somatic deep deletion of the wild-type BRCA2 allele in this tumor, superimposed on the known germline pathogenic variant (c.5946delT), confirms biallelic inactivation consistent with the Knudson two-hit hypothesis. This is a definitive HRR-loss event and the primary actionable biomarker.

2. Co-occurring TP53 + RB1 Loss — Lineage Plasticity Risk: The co-deletion of RB1 (Chr13q14.2, in close genomic proximity to BRCA2 at 13q12.3, suggesting possible coordinated chromosomal deletion event) combined with clonal TP53 p.R175H mutation creates the molecular substrate associated with an increased risk of treatment-induced neuroendocrine transdifferentiation (t-SCNC). This does not currently alter immediate management but warrants pathologic and clinical monitoring at future disease assessments. If clinical features of neuroendocrine differentiation emerge (loss of PSA expression with rising NSE or chromogranin A, rapidly progressive visceral disease), repeat biopsy with neuroendocrine marker assessment is advised.

3. AR Amplification and Enzalutamide Sequencing: The identification of high-level AR amplification at the time of mCRPC transition provides mechanistic context for abiraterone resistance and predicts a potentially abbreviated response to subsequent AR-pathway inhibition with enzalutamide. The clinical decision to proceed with enzalutamide is reasonable as a bridge strategy while monitoring PSA kinetics, with early consideration of olaparib as definitive BRCA2-targeted therapy.

4. Chromosomal 13q Co-deletion: The concurrent deep deletion of BRCA2 (13q12.3) and RB1 (13q14.2) suggests a broad chromosomal 13q deletion event, a well-recognized cytogenomic alteration in aggressive prostate cancer with dual tumor suppressor consequences.

5. Tumor Purity and Heterogeneity: Estimated tumor purity of 60% from decalcified bone biopsy material is satisfactory for reliable somatic variant calling. Decalcification protocols may introduce minor DNA quality artifacts; however, all key findings meet quality thresholds. Intratumoral heterogeneity cannot be fully excluded from a single metastatic site biopsy.

- AR splice variant detection (e.g., AR-V7) by NGS has limitations; dedicated assays (e.g., Oncotype DX AR-V7 Nucleus Detect, liquid biopsy) may provide additional information regarding AR splice variant status.
- Decalcification of bone specimens can affect DNA quality and may introduce artifacts; all reported findings met validated quality thresholds.
- PD-L1 IHC results may vary with tumor heterogeneity and specimen preparation; a single biopsy site may not be representative of the entire tumor PD-L1 landscape.

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Mr. Raymond Kowalski is a 71-year-old Polish-American male with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC), Gleason 9 (5+4), Grade Group 5, presenting today for his 3-month follow-up visit following initiation of enzalutamide. He comes in for PSA and toxicity assessment.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Kowalski was initially diagnosed approximately 3 years ago with de novo metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer (mHSPC), clinical stage T3bN1M1b, Gleason 9 (5+4), Grade Group 5 at age 68. His PSA at diagnosis was markedly elevated at 187.4 ng/mL. Staging workup demonstrated osseous metastases at L3, L4, right iliac crest, and right sacral ala on bone scintigraphy, as well as enlarged right external iliac lymph nodes up to 1.8 cm on CT, without visceral metastases. He was initiated on ADT with leuprolide acetate plus abiraterone acetate (Zytiga) 1000 mg daily with prednisone 5 mg twice daily, with bicalutamide flare prophylaxis at ADT initiation. He had an excellent initial response with PSA declining to a nadir of 0.8 ng/mL at 12 months. Germline testing performed during the hormone-sensitive phase identified a pathogenic BRCA2 frameshift variant (c.5946delT, p.Ser1982Argfs*22) in heterozygous state, confirming hereditary BRCA2 deficiency with implications for PARP inhibitor eligibility.

Approximately one year ago (at approximately 24 months on ADT + abiraterone), Mr. Kowalski demonstrated biochemical and eventual radiographic progression consistent with castration resistance. His PSA had risen to 8.6 ng/mL with a PSA doubling time of approximately 3.5 months, and testosterone confirmed castrate at < 10 ng/dL. Bone scintigraphy at that time was stable/sclerotic, and no visceral disease was identified. He was transitioned to enzalutamide (Xtandi) 160 mg orally daily approximately 3 months ago, with continuation of leuprolide acetate to maintain castrate testosterone.

Prior to initiating enzalutamide, a CT-guided biopsy of the L3 vertebral metastasis was performed, and somatic next-generation sequencing (NGS) was obtained. The tumor profile was notable for: BRCA2 biallelic loss (deep deletion with loss of heterozygosity of the germline allele — second-hit mechanism confirmed), TP53 p.R175H (pathogenic hotspot), AR amplification (approximately 8-fold copy number gain), RB1 homozygous deletion, PTEN heterozygous loss, and MYC amplification. Microsatellite status: MSS. TMB: 4 mutations/Mb (low). PD-L1 expression 1% (low). The AR amplification is notable as a potential mechanism of acquired AR-pathway therapy resistance going forward, while the BRCA2 biallelic loss provides a clear actionable target for PARP inhibitor therapy, with olaparib (Lynparza) identified as the next-line option pending current enzalutamide course.

Today, Mr. Kowalski returns 3 months after enzalutamide initiation. He reports overall improvement in his back pain since starting enzalutamide and states he is tolerating the medication reasonably well. He denies any new neurological symptoms. He reports ongoing mild fatigue and hot flashes, which he attributes to his longstanding ADT.

This represents a significant biochemical response to enzalutamide. No imaging has been obtained today per protocol (bone scan and CT deferred to 6-month restaging).

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Mr. Kowalski was initially diagnosed approximately 2 years ago with de novo metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer (mHSPC), Gleason 9 (5+4), Grade Group 5, cT3bN1M1b with bone metastases at L3, L4, right iliac crest, and right sacral ala, plus pelvic lymphadenopathy. His PSA at diagnosis was 187.4 ng/mL. He was initiated on ADT with leuprolide acetate plus abiraterone/prednisone (Zytiga) per the LATITUDE/STAMPEDE paradigm. He had a robust initial PSA response, reaching a nadir of 0.8 ng/mL at approximately 12 months.

His PSA subsequently rose, and by 24 months from initial therapy he had met criteria for castration resistance: three consecutive PSA rises above nadir with a confirmed castrate testosterone level below 10 ng/dL, with a PSA doubling time of approximately 3.5 months. He also developed new low back pain at that time. Germline genetic testing performed earlier in his course revealed a pathogenic BRCA2 frameshift variant (c.5946delT). Somatic tumor profiling of a CT-guided biopsy of the L3 vertebral metastasis confirmed BRCA2 biallelic loss (deep deletion, consistent with LOH of the germline allele plus somatic second hit), along with AR amplification (~8x), TP53 p.R175H, RB1 homozygous deletion, PTEN loss, and MYC amplification. MSI was stable and TMB was low at 4 mut/Mb. Notably, AR amplification was identified as a potential mechanism of resistance to prior AR-pathway therapy.

Given the above somatic landscape — specifically the AR amplification — and patient preference to continue on an oral AR-pathway agent before escalating to chemotherapy, the decision was made approximately 6 months ago to transition him from abiraterone/prednisone to enzalutamide 160 mg daily (Xtandi), with LHRH agonist continued. At his 3-month assessment, he had achieved a meaningful PSA decline from 8.6 to 2.3 ng/mL (approximately 73% reduction), with improvement in back pain to 1/10. He was tolerating enzalutamide well with only grade 1 fatigue and grade 1 hot flashes.

He now returns 6 months into enzalutamide therapy. Unfortunately, the PSA response has been lost. His most recent PSA today is 4.1 ng/mL, up from a nadir of approximately 1.9 ng/mL. His PSA doubling time over the past several measurements calculates to approximately 2.8 months, which is a concerning rate of rise. In addition, he now reports worsening low back pain, which he rates as 4/10 today (compared to 1/10 at his last visit), and new right hip pain that began approximately 3 to 4 weeks ago. He describes the hip pain as a deep, aching discomfort, rated 3/10 at rest but up to 5/10 with ambulation. He is not taking any opioids but has been using naproxen with only partial relief.

He denies any new neurologic symptoms — no leg weakness, no bowel or bladder dysfunction, no saddle anesthesia. He denies fever, chills. He reports mild fatigue (grade 1-2) and reduced exercise tolerance compared to 3 months ago. No new urinary symptoms. He does note a slight decrease in appetite but no significant weight loss since last visit. He denies hematuria, hemoptysis, or abdominal pain.

The pattern of rising PSA with a brisk doubling time, new bone pain involving the right hip, and pain in a site consistent with the right iliac crest/femoral region (a known area of prior metastatic disease) raises significant concern for biochemical and early clinical/radiographic progression on enzalutamide. Given his BRCA2 biallelic loss, the planned next step — if imaging confirms progression — is transition to olaparib (Lynparza), which carries FDA approval for HRR-deficient mCRPC based on the PROfound trial data showing PFS and OS benefit specifically in BRCA1/2-altered patients.

Importantly, while AR amplification identified on somatic NGS provides a potential mechanism for enzalutamide resistance, the BRCA2 biallelic loss remains the most actionable alteration at this juncture, making PARP inhibitor therapy the preferred next line.

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Mr. Raymond Kowalski is a 71-year-old male with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer harboring germline and somatic BRCA2 biallelic loss, presenting today for follow-up approximately 8 months into his olaparib (Lynparza) course. He is being seen following hospitalization-level management of Grade 3 anemia and associated transfusion, with today's visit focused on reassessment of blood counts, symptom burden, disease status, and safe re-initiation of olaparib at a further reduced dose.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Kowalski was initially diagnosed approximately 3 years ago with Stage IVB prostatic adenocarcinoma, Gleason 9 (5+4), Grade Group 5, with bone metastases at L3, L4, right iliac crest, and right sacral ala, as well as pelvic lymph node involvement (right external iliac). PSA at diagnosis was 187.4 ng/mL. He was started on androgen deprivation therapy with leuprolide (Lupron Depot 22.5 mg IM q3 months) combined with abiraterone acetate (Zytiga) 1000 mg daily plus prednisone 5 mg BID, achieving an excellent initial PSA response to nadir of 0.8 ng/mL at roughly 12 months.

Unfortunately, PSA began rising at approximately 18 months and biochemical CRPC was confirmed at the 24-month mark, with PSA 8.6 ng/mL on castrate testosterone. At that time, somatic tumor profiling (CT-guided biopsy of the L3 vertebral metastasis) confirmed BRCA2 biallelic loss (consistent with germline BRCA2 c.5946delT frameshift variant plus somatic second-hit deletion), along with AR amplification (~8x), TP53 p.R175H, and RB1 homozygous deletion. He was transitioned to enzalutamide (Xtandi) 160 mg daily, which yielded a modest PSA response (nadir 1.9 ng/mL) but proved short-lived; radiographic progression with new bone lesions (right femoral neck, T11, left acetabulum) and new PSMA-avid pelvic lymph nodes was documented at approximately 7 months. Denosumab (Prolia/Xgeva 120 mg SQ q4 weeks) was started alongside bone-protective supplementation at that time.

Olaparib (Lynparza) 300 mg BID was initiated approximately 8 months ago given the confirmed BRCA2 biallelic somatic loss (PROfound trial FDA-approved indication). PSA at olaparib start was 18.2 ng/mL. By 3 months, PSA had declined to 5.7 ng/mL (69% reduction) with substantial symptomatic improvement in bone pain. Imaging at 6 months demonstrated partial bone scan response with decreased tracer uptake in right femoral neck, T11, and left acetabulum lesions, and right pelvic lymph nodes shrank from 0.8 cm to 0.4 cm. PSA at 6 months was 3.9 ng/mL.

However, beginning at approximately 4 months on olaparib, Mr. Kowalski developed progressive anemia: hemoglobin dropped from a pre-treatment baseline of 12.4 g/dL to 9.1 g/dL (Grade 2) at 4 months, prompting dose reduction from 300 mg BID to 200 mg BID. At 7 months, hemoglobin deteriorated further to 7.4 g/dL (Grade 3), accompanied by Grade 1 thrombocytopenia (platelets 128 K/uL), marked symptomatic fatigue, exertional dyspnea, and lightheadedness. He received 2 units of packed red blood cells (pRBC) in the infusion center, with post-transfusion hemoglobin recovering to 9.8 g/dL. Olaparib was held at that time. Hematology was consulted; bone marrow biopsy was deferred given the clear clinical context of PARP inhibitor-related myelosuppression. A plan was formulated to restart olaparib at a further reduced dose of 150 mg BID once hemoglobin stabilized above 9 g/dL.

Today, Mr. Kowalski presents approximately 3-4 weeks after that transfusion and olaparib hold. He reports his energy has improved considerably compared to the time of his transfusion, though he remains more fatigued than his pre-olaparib baseline. He denies active dyspnea at rest and notes he can walk short distances without significant breathlessness. Bone pain remains relatively well controlled at 1-2/10, which he attributes to the ongoing disease response. He has not had fevers, chills, or signs of infection. No new bleeding, bruising beyond mild, or neurological symptoms. He reports no chest pain or palpitations. He continues calcium 500 mg and vitamin D 400 IU daily with his denosumab.

Social context: Retired ironworker, lives with his wife. His daughter, who was informed of the germline BRCA2 finding, has completed cascade testing and was found to carry the same variant; she is being followed at an outside high-risk cancer program. Mr. Kowalski's wife accompanies him today and remains closely involved in his care.

ASSESSMENT

Reticulocyte count is rebounding, suggesting endogenous erythropoietic recovery is beginning. The myelosuppressive pattern here — macrocytic anemia with low reticulocyte count, normal B12/folate, no iron deficiency — is entirely consistent with PARP inhibitor-related bone marrow suppression; no alternative etiology has been identified.

4. He was transitioned to enzalutamide (Xtandi) 160 mg daily as second-line AR pathway inhibitor therapy. Olaparib was initiated approximately 11 months ago. - All lesions strongly PSMA-avid, confirming PSMA-expressing disease burden

ASSESSMENT:

1. Metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) -- BRCA2 germline (c.5946delT) and somatic biallelic loss, TP53 p.R175H, AR amplification, RB1 loss -- now with confirmed visceral disease progression on olaparib (Lynparza) after approximately 11 months of therapy. PSA 31.7 ng/mL. Disease has spread to liver and lung (new visceral metastases), in addition to progressive bone and lymph node burden. This represents a qualitatively more aggressive disease course with visceral involvement, which carries a less favorable prognosis. Prior therapies: ADT + abiraterone (first-line mHSPC, approximately 24 months), enzalutamide (second-line mCRPC, 7 months), olaparib (third-line, 11 months with initial response and subsequent progression).

2. Treatment-related myelosuppression -- persistent Grade 2 anemia (Hgb 8.8 g/dL) and Grade 1 thrombocytopenia (platelets 92 K/uL) in the setting of olaparib-associated bone marrow toxicity, compounded by bone marrow replacement from extensive osseous metastatic disease. Multiple prior dose reductions of olaparib; olaparib currently held in the setting of progression and myelosuppression workup. Two prior packed red cell transfusions. On darbepoetin alfa with suboptimal response. LFTs mildly elevated, likely combination of hepatic metastasis and residual marrow/nutritional effects.

4. Nutritional compromise -- 8-lb unintentional weight loss over 3 months, albumin 3.3 g/dL, reduced appetite. Consistent with progressive disease and treatment-related factors. Will require nutritional support and close monitoring.

PLAN:

1. Discontinue olaparib. Given clear visceral disease progression on PSMA PET/CT, continued administration of olaparib at any dose is not clinically appropriate. Olaparib will be formally discontinued as of today. The patient and family were counseled regarding the rationale for stopping therapy and the transition to a new treatment approach.

2. Next-line treatment discussion. An extensive goals-of-care conversation was held today with Mr. Kowalski and his family (daughter present). We discussed his disease course, current status, and realistic expectations for further treatment. The following options were reviewed in detail:

   a. Platinum-based chemotherapy: Given his BRCA2-deficient tumor biology, there is biological rationale and retrospective/emerging prospective data supporting platinum sensitivity in HRR-deficient mCRPC. A regimen of carboplatin (dosed to AUC 4-5) combined with docetaxel (Taxotere) is the most commonly used platinum-based doublet in this setting. This approach would target both the BRCA2-related HR deficiency and provide systemic cytotoxic coverage of visceral disease. Potential toxicities include myelosuppression (particularly relevant given his prior anemia history), peripheral neuropathy (he has mild pre-existing paresthesias), alopecia, nausea, fatigue, and risk of febrile neutropenia. G-CSF support would be employed prophylactically. Given his visceral crisis risk from new hepatic metastases, this is the most urgently indicated active cytotoxic regimen.

   b. Lu-177-PSMA-617 (lutetium vipivotide tetraxetan; Pluvicto): Mr. Kowalski's disease is strongly PSMA-avid on recent imaging (all lesions PSMA-positive, no significant PSMA-negative sites), making him technically eligible for Lu-177 PSMA therapy per VISION trial criteria (post-AR pathway therapy and post-taxane, though he has not received prior docetaxel yet in this case - to be clarified regarding eligibility per current label). Lu-177 PSMA-617 has demonstrated survival benefit in PSMA-positive mCRPC. However, given the presence of new visceral metastases (liver and lung) and more rapidly evolving disease, the panel response rate of Lu-177 may not be sufficient to control visceral progression in the immediate term. Additionally, his persistent myelosuppression may be exacerbated by radioligand therapy. This option may be more appropriate after platinum-based chemotherapy if disease remains PSMA-avid and performance status permits.

   c. Cabazitaxel (Jevtana): While approved for post-docetaxel mCRPC, Mr. Kowalski has not received prior docetaxel. Some data support cabazitaxel as a taxane option. However, given the availability of the carboplatin/docetaxel combination leveraging his platinum sensitivity, and his prior myelosuppression history, cabazitaxel is not preferred at this juncture.

   d. Clinical trial: Mr. Kowalski should be evaluated for clinical trial eligibility, particularly trials targeting BRCA2-mutant mCRPC with visceral involvement, combined PARP inhibitor + platinum combinations, or novel AR-pathway combinations. Tumor board discussion will be sought.

   e. Best supportive care / palliative focus: This was discussed openly. Given ECOG PS 2 with recent trajectory toward PS 3, and the burden of treatment over 3 years, the option of transitioning to purely symptom-directed palliative care without further systemic therapy is valid and respected. Mr. Kowalski expressed a strong desire to pursue further active treatment if his body can tolerate it, and he understood the risks and benefits. He and his family feel that the potential for tumor response and extended time justifies the toxicity risk at this point.

   Decision: After thorough discussion, we will proceed with carboplatin + docetaxel chemotherapy as the most appropriate next-line treatment given: (a) visceral crisis risk from hepatic metastases, (b) BRCA2-driven platinum sensitivity rationale, (c) patient's desire for continued active treatment, and (d) PSMA-directed therapy preserved as a future option. We will target carboplatin AUC 4 and docetaxel 60 mg/m2 every 21 days, with prophylactic G-CSF (filgrastim) given his prior myelosuppression history and ECOG PS 2.

3. Supportive care and referrals:
   - Palliative care consultation: Placed urgently given evolving prognosis, pain management needs, and family support requirements. Palliative care team will be actively co-managing symptom burden.
   - Nutrition/dietitian referral: For unintentional weight loss and declining albumin; discuss nutritional supplementation strategies.
   - Bone protective therapy: Denosumab 120 mg SQ every 4 weeks to be continued (last dose administered 3 weeks ago; next dose on schedule). Calcium and vitamin D supplementation continued.
   - Leuprolide acetate (LHRH agonist): Continued at 22.5 mg IM every 3 months to maintain castrate testosterone; next injection due in 6 weeks.
   - Pain management: Scheduled non-opioid analgesics (acetaminophen) with breakthrough opioid (oxycodone 5 mg PO PRN) prescribed for bone pain management. Pain team involvement if escalation needed.
   - Darbepoetin alfa: Will continue at current dosing schedule pending chemotherapy initiation (to be reassessed; ESA use may be adjusted once chemotherapy-related anemia context is re-established).
   - Social work: Engaged for transportation, caregiver support, and community resources.
   - CBC and CMP: Recheck in 1-2 weeks prior to chemotherapy initiation (cycle 1). Platelet recovery to >100 K/uL and Hgb stability required before starting carboplatin.

4. Advance care planning: The patient has a healthcare proxy designated (daughter, present today). We reviewed his current advance directive. He wishes for full resuscitation measures at this time but has documented a preference for comfort-focused care if he progresses to a state where he is no longer able to meaningfully interact with family. ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. METASTATIC CASTRATION-RESISTANT PROSTATE CANCER (mCRPC), BRCA2 biallelic loss, progressive disease:

Mr. Kowalski has completed 4 cycles of carboplatin/docetaxel (dose-reduced docetaxel 55 mg/m2, carboplatin AUC 4). After initial encouraging PSA decline and partial hepatic response (confirmed at 2-cycle restaging CT), his PSA has now risen from 12.1 to 19.8 ng/mL -- a concerning upward trend that strongly suggests loss of chemotherapy efficacy. This is consistent with the aggressive biology of his tumor (AR amplification, TP53 and RB1 loss, PTEN loss in addition to BRCA2 biallelic loss), which portends a short-lived response window even to platinum-based therapy.

I have discussed the PSA trajectory with Mr. Kowalski and his family at length today. Restaging imaging has been ordered; results are anticipated within the next 3-4 days and we will review them together in a scheduled phone call or brief office visit. If imaging confirms radiographic progression -- which the clinical trajectory and PSA kinetics make likely -- we will need to discuss next therapeutic steps.

Treatment options reviewed in detail with patient and family today:

a) Lutetium-177 PSMA-617 (Lu-177-PSMA-617; Pluvicto): This is our primary consideration and the option most likely to offer meaningful benefit at this juncture, should imaging confirm PSMA-avid progressive disease. His prior PSMA PET/CT (obtained at time of progression on olaparib, approximately 3 months ago) demonstrated strongly PSMA-avid disease at all metastatic sites with high SUVmax values. All lesions including hepatic and bone were PSMA-avid, making him potentially eligible for Lu-177-PSMA-617 based on VISION trial criteria. We discussed that this is an FDA-approved, nuclear medicine-administered therapy, given IV every 6 weeks for up to 6 cycles, with manageable toxicity (mainly fatigue, dry mouth, nausea, and hematologic effects). We discussed realistic expectations: approximately half of patients see PSA responses, and the therapy can meaningfully extend life and improve bone pain in PSMA-avid disease. HOWEVER, I emphasized that his current performance status (ECOG 2-3 today, functionally requiring assistance with some ADLs) is a critical eligibility consideration that will need to be reassessed after imaging. I have placed a referral to nuclear medicine / theranostics for evaluation pending imaging results.

b) Cabazitaxel (Jevtana): As a third-line taxane (this would technically be his second taxane exposure, having received docetaxel), cabazitaxel is an option for mCRPC post-docetaxel. However, given his current ECOG PS of 2-3, cumulative Grade 2-3 peripheral neuropathy (worsening, with new hand involvement and near-fall), ongoing anemia, and thrombocytopenia, the risk-benefit calculus is unfavorable. Cabazitaxel would be expected to worsen neuropathy and myelosuppression substantially. I do not recommend this option at the current time unless performance status improves significantly.

c) Clinical trial: I reviewed our active clinical trial portfolio. There may be opportunities for trials targeting AR-pathway resistance or DNA damage response. I will review available studies and discuss at next visit.

d) Best supportive care and hospice: This option was honestly and compassionately discussed. Given his trajectory -- Stage IV disease with liver and bone involvement, ECOG PS 2-3, progressive disease through four prior treatment lines (ADT+abiraterone, enzalutamide, olaparib, platinum/taxane doublet), falling albumin, significant weight loss, and worsening functional status -- a transition to hospice with comfort-focused care is a medically appropriate and potentially preferred path for some patients. We discussed that hospice does not mean "giving up" but rather focusing on quality of life, symptom management, and being home with family. His family was present and engaged in this conversation. Mr. Kowalski acknowledged understanding and expressed that he wants to see the scan results before making any final decisions. He is not ready for hospice at this time but appreciated the candor.

Plan: Order restaging PSMA PET/CT + CT chest/abdomen/pelvis (ordered today). Carboplatin/docetaxel held pending imaging given PSA re-elevation and ECOG decline. Referral to nuclear medicine/theranostics for Lu-177-PSMA-617 evaluation placed. Follow-up call scheduled in 3-4 days for imaging results review. Goals of care conversation documented; advance directive discussion ongoing.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. METASTATIC HORMONE-SENSITIVE PROSTATE CANCER (mHSPC), STAGE IVB, GLEASON 9 (5+4) — MAJOR BIOCHEMICAL RESPONSE TO ADT + abi AT 3 MONTHS

Mr. Kowalski has had an outstanding early response to intensified ADT. His PSA has fallen from 187.4 ng/mL at diagnosis to 4.2 ng/mL today, a 97.8% reduction achieved in just 3 months. This depth of PSA response is clinically meaningful — patients achieving PSA <4 ng/mL or greater than 90% decline at 3–6 months on combined ADT therapy in the mHSPC setting have demonstrated favorable outcomes in multiple landmark datasets, including analyses from LATITUDE and STAMPEDE. Testosterone is confirmed in the deeply castrate range at <10 ng/dL, confirming the adequacy of LHRH agonist therapy and ruling out castration failure as a confounding factor. Clinically, bone pain has improved from 7/10 to 2/10, performance status is maintained at ECOG 1, and alkaline phosphatase has normalized, all convergent indicators of meaningful disease response.

Plan:
  - Continue leup acetate (Lupron Depot) 22.5 mg IM every 3 months; confirm and schedule next injection date
  - Continue abi acetate (Zytiga) 1000 mg orally once daily on empty stomach; reinforce dietary instructions (fasting critical for bioavailability — taking with food dramatically reduces absorption and efficacy)
  - Continue prednisone 5 mg orally twice daily concurrently with abi; patient reminded not to abruptly discontinue
  - PSA nadir expected to continue declining over coming months; nadir typically reached between 6 and 12 months of therapy
  - Repeat PSA, testosterone, and CMP (including potassium, liver enzymes) in 6 weeks
  - Formal restaging imaging (Tc-99m bone scan + CT chest/abdomen/pelvis) scheduled at 12-month mark; will proceed sooner if there is clinical suspicion of progression (new or worsening bone pain, rising PSA on two consecutive measurements, or new symptoms)

2. abi/PREDNISONE TOLERABILITY AND SAFETY MONITORING

The patient is tolerating the current regimen well with no significant adverse effects at this point. Key safety parameters reviewed:
  - Hypokalemia: None (K+ 4.1 mEq/L). Zytiga inhibits CYP17A1, leading to accumulation of upstream mineralocorticoid precursors that can cause hypokalemia and fluid retention; prednisone co-administration partially mitigates this. Monthly potassium monitoring to continue.
  - Hepatotoxicity: None (AST 22, ALT 28, all within normal limits). Liver enzyme monitoring per schedule (monthly for first 3 months, then every 3 months thereafter if stable).
  - Fluid retention/hypertension: Mild bilateral ankle edema unchanged from baseline; blood pressure well controlled on lisinopril. No medication changes needed at this time.
  - Hyperglycemia: None on prednisone (glucose 96 fasting). Continued monitoring with CMP.
  - Hot flushes: Moderate frequency (5–8/day), partially managed with venlafaxine 37.5 mg. Consider titrating venlafaxine to 75 mg daily at next visit if hot flashes remain bothersome; alternatively, can discuss acupuncture or medroxyprogesterone as adjuncts.

3. BONE HEALTH

Patient has pre-existing bilateral mild osteopenia identified on DEXA scan obtained at time of ADT initiation. Prolonged ADT accelerates bone mineral density loss (approximately 2–3% per year), and he additionally carries bone metastatic disease at multiple sites (L3, L4, right iliac crest, right sacral ala). Currently on calcium carbonate 1200 mg/day and vitamin D 1000 IU/day as baseline supplementation. The role of bone-modifying agents (zoledronic acid or denosumab) in the hormone-sensitive metastatic setting is evolving; in the CRPC setting, denosumab 120 mg every 4 weeks is FDA-approved for skeletal event prevention. Will revisit this at next visit with updated guideline review. Ensure vitamin D sufficiency at next labs. Advise continued weight-bearing activity as tolerated.

4. GERMLINE GENETIC COUNSELING — REFERRAL PLACED

Given the patient's diagnosis with high-grade, metastatic prostate cancer at age 68 and notable family history (father: prostate cancer; paternal aunt: ovarian cancer), referral to genetic counseling has been formally placed. Per current NCCN, AUA, and ESMO guidelines, germline testing for BRCA1/2, ATM, CDK12, CHEK2, and other HRR-pathway genes is recommended for patients with metastatic, high-grade, or early-onset prostate cancer. Identification of a germline BRCA2 or other HRR pathogenic variant would have implications both for the patient's own future treatment options (PARP inhibitor eligibility, platinum sensitivity) and for at-risk family members who should undergo cascade genetic testing. Patient is aware of this referral and is agreeable.

5. GOALS OF CARE AND LONGITUDINAL PLANNING

At this stage, the disease is demonstrating a strong response to first-line intensified ADT and the prognosis in mHSPC remains guarded (median OS approximately 3–4 years with high-risk features and Zytiga-based therapy in LATITUDE, though individual variation is significant). Patient and wife appear well informed and engaged. No urgent goals-of-care discussions required at this visit given excellent response and maintained functional status. Will continue longitudinal counseling. At such time as the disease becomes castration-resistant — defined biochemically as three consecutive PSA rises from nadir on castrate testosterone, or radiographic progression — treatment re-stratification will be necessary. In the event of castration resistance, options including second-line AR pathway inhibition (enza or darolutamide), PARP inhibitors (dependent on HRR gene mutation status), taxane chemotherapy, or other approved agents would be considered. The current excellent response is the first priority.

6. SUPPORTIVE CARE AND QUALITY OF LIFE

Patient is independent in ADLs, living at home, and physically active. He has been counseled on the importance of aerobic exercise and resistance training during ADT to mitigate the well-documented metabolic and musculoskeletal sequelae of testosterone suppression (sarcopenia, osteopenia, insulin resistance, fatigue, and cardiovascular risk). VARIANT INTERPRETATION:
The identified variant, BRCA2 c.5946delT (p.Ser1982Argfs*22), is a single-nucleotide deletion in exon 11 of the BRCA2 gene, resulting in a frameshift that introduces a premature stop codon 22 amino acids downstream of the alteration (p.Ser1982Argfs*22). This variant is predicted to produce a truncated and/or unstable BRCA2 protein via nonsense-mediated mRNA decay, resulting in loss of function of the BRCA2 protein. BRCA2 encodes a critical tumor suppressor protein involved in homologous recombination-mediated DNA double-strand break repair. Loss of BRCA2 function leads to genomic instability and predisposition to multiple cancer types.

This specific variant (c.5946delT) is a well-characterized, recurrently identified pathogenic variant in the literature and in the ClinVar database, with classification supported by:
  - Evidence of pathogenicity from multiple independent laboratories (ClinVar multiple submitters, consensus classification: Pathogenic)
  - Frameshift nature resulting in premature termination codon (null allele)
  - Co-segregation with disease in affected families reported in the literature
  - Absent or extremely rare frequency in general population databases (gnomAD)
  - Strong prior probability based on variant type and location within a functionally critical domain (OB-fold domain region, critical for RAD51 interaction and HRR activity)

CLINICAL INTERPRETATION SUMMARY

This germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant (c.5946delT, p.Ser1982Argfs*22) is identified in the heterozygous state in this patient with metastatic high-grade prostate adenocarcinoma (Gleason 9, Stage IVB). Germline BRCA2 pathogenic variants are associated with a substantially elevated lifetime risk for the following malignancies:
  - Prostate cancer: Estimated cumulative lifetime risk 19-34% (vs. ~12% population risk); associated with higher grade disease, earlier onset, and more aggressive clinical behavior
  - Breast cancer (male): Elevated lifetime risk (~7-8%)
  - Breast cancer (female, in female relatives): Approximately 47-72% lifetime risk
  - Ovarian/fallopian tube/peritoneal cancer (in female relatives): Approximately 11-17% lifetime risk
  - Pancreatic cancer: Approximately 2-7% lifetime risk (2-6x relative risk above general population)
  - Melanoma (uveal and cutaneous): Modestly elevated risk

First-degree and second-degree relatives of this patient carry a 50% a priori probability of harboring this same heterozygous BRCA2 pathogenic variant. Cascade genetic testing of at-risk relatives is strongly recommended.

With respect to the patient's current cancer diagnosis: The identification of a germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant in the setting of metastatic castration-sensitive prostate cancer has direct therapeutic implications. BRCA2-mutant prostate cancers demonstrate loss of homologous recombination repair (HRR) capacity, rendering tumors potentially sensitive to PARP inhibitors (e.g., olaparib, rucaparib) upon progression to castration resistance. Somatic tumor testing (e.g., liquid or tissue-based comprehensive genomic profiling) is recommended to evaluate for biallelic inactivation of BRCA2 (second somatic hit/LOH), which would confirm tumor-level HRR deficiency and support PARP inhibitor eligibility per FDA-approved indications.

Mr. Kowalski has developed castration-resistant prostate cancer by standard criteria (PCWGx): three rising PSA values above nadir (PSA now 8.6 ng/mL, up from nadir of 0.8 ng/mL), with confirmed castrate testosterone <10 ng/dL, representing failure of first-line intensified androgen deprivation therapy with abiraterone. This is not unexpected — the median progression-free survival for mHSPC on ADT plus abiraterone (LATITUDE, STAMPEDE data) is approximately 24 months, and he has now reached that landmark.

His germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant (confirmed ~12 months ago) and the pending somatic tumor profile are critically relevant to next-line therapy selection. BRCA2 biallelic loss, if confirmed somatically (as is expected given the germline variant and typical tumor biology in this setting), renders him eligible for olaparib (Lynparza) per the PROfound trial FDA-approved indication. However, we will await the NGS results before finalizing next-line therapy.

Key considerations in treatment selection:
- Prior abiraterone use: Cross-resistance with enzalutamide exists to some degree, but sequential AR pathway inhibitor (ARPI) use is commonly employed in clinical practice. Given that his abiraterone therapy lasted 24 months with a prolonged PSA response, a further AR pathway-targeted approach with enzalutamide is reasonable.
- BRCA2 germline/somatic mutation: This is a tier 1 actionable alteration. PARP inhibitor (olaparib 300 mg BID) is FDA-approved in this setting (post-one ARPI), but preferring to sequence enzalutamide first is defensible and consistent with patient preference to defer chemotherapy-associated options; olaparib reserved as second-line within mCRPC.
- ECOG PS: Currently 1. Patient is a reasonable candidate for most available options.
- Renal function: eGFR 52 mL/min — relevant for agent selection and dose considerations going forward. Olaparib does not require dose adjustment at this GFR level.
- Patient preference: Reviewed treatment options thoroughly with Mr. Kowalski and his daughter. He understands the disease has progressed and is no longer being controlled by current therapy. He strongly prefers oral options and would like to defer intravenous chemotherapy for as long as possible. He is comfortable with continued monitoring and does not want to rush into aggressive treatment.

Plan:
- Discontinue abiraterone acetate 1000 mg daily (last dose today). Taper prednisone 5 mg BID — reduce to 5 mg daily for 1 week, then discontinue (to allow HPA axis recovery after prolonged low-dose corticosteroid exposure).
- Initiate enzalutamide (Xtandi) 160 mg orally once daily (4 × 40 mg capsules). Reviewed key side effects: fatigue, hot flashes, hypertension, dizziness/fall risk, and the rare but important risk of seizure (<1%; patient counseled to avoid activities risking significant injury if seizure occurs). He has no prior seizure history, no history of traumatic brain injury, and takes no medications that lower seizure threshold. Instructed to take with or without food; avoid concomitant CYP enzyme inducers.
- Continue leuprolide acetate 22.5 mg IM every 3 months to maintain castrate testosterone — next injection due in approximately 6 weeks (schedule confirmed with nursing).
- Baseline labs prior to enzalutamide initiation confirmed adequate today (CBC, CMP as above).
- Await somatic NGS results (expected within 7-10 days); results will be reviewed with patient at next visit and will inform sequencing of future therapy (PARP inhibitor consideration after enzalutamide).
- Repeat bone scan (Tc-99m whole body scintigraphy) and CT chest/abdomen/pelvis ordered to establish new mCRPC baseline — patient will complete these studies within the next 2 weeks.

2. LOW BACK PAIN — NEW, RELATED TO KNOWN SPINAL METASTASES

New low back pain at L3-L4 level is most consistent with known osseous metastatic disease at those sites in the setting of biochemical progression. Neurological examination is reassuring — full motor strength, intact sensation, no signs of cord compression.

- Continue acetaminophen 1000 mg TID scheduled (not to exceed 3 g/day; counseled to avoid additional OTC acetaminophen-containing products).
- Added oxycodone IR 5 mg every 4-6 hours as needed for breakthrough pain (not to exceed 4 doses per 24 hours); patient instructed on constipation prophylaxis (docusate sodium 100 mg BID, senna 8.6 mg BID) given opioid initiation.
- If pain worsens, or if any neurological symptoms develop (particularly lower extremity weakness, changes in bladder/bowel), patient instructed to go to the emergency department immediately given risk for epidural spinal cord compression (ESCC).
- Pain and spinal status will be reassessed at next visit. Radiation oncology referral for palliative radiation to lumbar spine will be considered if pain not adequately controlled with current measures or if neurological concern arises.
- Bone-modifying agent therapy: Given transition to mCRPC with known bone metastases, initiating a bone-modifying agent was discussed. Zoledronic acid (Zometa) would be standard but GFR of 52 mL/min warrants caution with bisphosphonates; denosumab (Xgeva) 120 mg subcutaneous every 4 weeks is the preferred option in this patient given his renal function. Will initiate denosumab at next visit once imaging is reviewed and mCRPC diagnosis is formally staged. Calcium 500 mg with vitamin D 400 IU daily supplementation instructed to begin today in anticipation.

3. GERMLINE BRCA2 — ONGOING MANAGEMENT

Patient carrying germline BRCA2 c.5946delT (heterozygous, pathogenic). Somatic biallelic loss is expected on the pending tumor NGS (second hit via LOH/somatic deletion). Family cascade testing is underway (one daughter has been seen in genetic counseling). No further germline action needed at this time.

4. CHRONIC KIDNEY DISEASE, STAGE 3A (eGFR 52 mL/min)

Likely multifactorial (age, baseline, possible contrast exposure history). Stable from prior checks. Will continue to monitor creatinine monthly. Avoid nephrotoxic agents where possible. NSAIDs explicitly avoided.

5. ADT-RELATED EFFECTS

Patient has been on continuous ADT for approximately 24 months. He has baseline grade 1 fatigue and hot flashes. Anemia (Hgb 12.1 g/dL) is mild and consistent with ADT-related erythropoietic suppression. No osteoporosis-related fracture to date. DEXA scan should be ordered given 24 months of ADT — if osteopenia or osteoporosis identified, denosumab (planned above) will serve dual purpose. Patient counseled on fall precautions, calcium/vitamin D supplementation (initiated above).

FOLLOW-UP

Mr. Kowalski will follow up in 3 weeks to review: (1) somatic NGS tumor profiling results, (2) repeat bone scan and CT staging images, and (3) initial tolerability of enzalutamide. Prescription for enzalutamide provided to the specialty pharmacy; patient counseled that a prior authorization process may take 3-5 business days. He will call the triage nurse line if he experiences any new neurological symptoms, severe pain, falls, fever, or other urgent concerns prior to his next scheduled visit.

Patient and his daughter verbalized understanding of today's discussion including the diagnosis of castration-resistant prostate cancer, the transition in therapy, and the next steps. Questions were answered. He is coping reasonably well and remains engaged in his care. Palliative care has not yet been formally involved but will be considered in the near future as disease burden increases.

ECOG Performance Status: 1
Total visit time: 55 minutes (>50% of which was counseling and coordination of care).
